grammarLessons.name: Les Adverbes de Quantité — beaucoup, peu, assez

cards.grammarNote:
**l'eau** is feminine. Use the partitive **de l'eau** for an unspecified amount: Je bois de l'eau.
A definite quantity uses un verre d'eau, une bouteille d'eau, etc.
The partitive (**du** / **de la** / **de l'**) is essential with food and drink.

**le pain** is masculine. Use the partitive **du pain** for an unspecified amount: **Je voudrais du pain**.
After negation the partitive becomes **de**: **je ne mange pas de pain**.
French bread culture: **une baguette**, **un pain de campagne** (country loaf), **une ficelle** (thin baguette).

**le riz** is masculine. The z is silent in **le riz** but can be heard in liaison before a vowel: **du riz_au lait** (rice pudding).
Partitive: **du riz** (some rice) — not *un riz* because rice is uncountable.
Adjective agreement: **des légumes** (plural) — the verb **aimer** with a definite article means a general preference.
cards.phrase: J'aime le riz avec des légumes.

cards.grammarNote:
**le lait** is masculine (from Latin **lac/lactis**, root of English 'lactose').
Partitive: **du lait** — **Tu veux du lait ?** asks 'do you want some milk?'
After negation: **je ne veux pas de lait** (no article after **de** in negation).

Singular: **l'œuf** — the f is pronounced.
Plural: **les œufs** — the f goes silent! Pronounced 'leh-ZUH'. A classic French pronunciation trap.
cards.englishPhrase: I eat two eggs for breakfast.
cards.phrase: Je mange deux œufs au petit-déjeuner.

cards.grammarNote:
**le fromage** is masculine. Use partitive **du fromage** for an unspecified quantity.
France has over 1,000 cheese varieties — **le fromage** is culturally significant.
**a du fromage** in the example — this is the partitive, meaning the sandwich contains some cheese (not a whole cheese).

**le poulet** = chicken (as food). As a live animal: **la poule** (hen) or **le coq** (rooster).
Definite article **le** here: **le poulet est délicieux** — a general statement about this chicken.
Partitive for unspecified amount: **Je mange du poulet** (I'm eating [some] chicken).

**la viande** is feminine. Partitive would be **de la viande**.
Negation changes partitive to **de**: **il ne mange pas de viande** — the article drops after **ne…pas**.
This rule applies to all partitives and indefinite articles in negation: **du pain** → **pas de pain**, **une pomme** → **pas de pomme**.
cards.phrase: Il ne mange pas de viande.

cards.grammarNote:
**le poisson** = fish (food or animal). **la poissonnerie** = fishmonger's shop.
**J'aime le poisson** — the definite article **le** with **aimer** expresses a general preference, not a specific fish.
Nasal vowel: **poisson** contains the nasal **on** — pronounced like the end of 'song' in English.

**la baguette** is feminine — literally 'a little rod/wand' (same word used for a conductor's baton and magic wand).
**Je voudrais** = I would like — the conditional of **vouloir**, used for polite requests.
**une baguette** uses the indefinite article (one specific baguette), not the partitive.
cards.phrase: Je voudrais une baguette, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**le croissant** = crescent (its shape). From French **croître** (to grow), related to the crescent moon shape.
**Je prends** (I'm having/taking) is a common way to order at a café — more casual than **je voudrais**.
**au beurre** = with butter — **au** is **à + le** (contraction). A **croissant au beurre** is made with real butter (richer and flakier than a plain croissant).
cards.phrase: Je prends un croissant au beurre.

cards.grammarNote:
**la crêpe** is feminine. The circumflex on the **ê** indicates a historical 's' (Old French: **crespe**) — compare English 'crisp'.
**au chocolat** = with chocolate — **au** is **à + le** (**le chocolat** is masculine).
**Je veux** here is direct — in a casual context acceptable. For a shop, **je voudrais** is more polite.
cards.phrase: Je veux une crêpe au chocolat.

cards.grammarNote:
**le porc** = pork (food). The c is silent: pronounced 'por'.
Negation: **je ne mange pas de porc** — the partitive **du** drops to **de** after **ne…pas**.
Animal: **le cochon** or **le porc** — in English 'pig' and 'pork' differ (Germanic vs. Latin roots); French uses **le porc** for both.
cards.phrase: Je ne mange pas de porc.

cards.grammarNote:
**le fruit** (singular) / **les fruits** (plural). **Des fruits** = the partitive/indefinite plural for some fruit.
**Tous les jours** = every day — **tous** (m. pl.) agrees with **les jours** (m. pl.). Compare: **toutes les semaines** (f. pl.).
In English 'fruit' is typically uncountable; in French, individual fruits are counted: **un fruit**, **deux fruits**.
cards.phrase: Je mange des fruits tous les jours.

cards.grammarNote:
**la pomme** is feminine. Literally means 'apple' but appears in: **la pomme de terre** (potato — lit. 'apple of the earth') and **la pomme d'Adam** (Adam's apple).
**par jour** = per day — **par** is used for rates and frequencies.
**la santé** = health (feminine). The phrase **c'est bon pour la santé** is very commonly used.
cards.englishPhrase: An apple a day is healthy.
cards.phrase: Une pomme par jour, c'est bon pour la santé.

cards.grammarNote:
**la banane** is feminine. An easy gender rule: most nouns ending in **-ane** are feminine.
**Les bananes sont mûres** — adjective **mûr** agrees with the feminine plural noun: **mûres**.
In French slang, **une banane** can mean a big grin (un grand sourire en forme de banane).

**l'orange** is feminine — unusual because words ending in a consonant sound are often masculine.
**jus d'orange** — note no article after **de** when combining nouns: **jus de** + orange (not *jus de l'orange*).
**Je veux un jus d'orange** — here **un jus** = one glass/serving of juice (countable).

**le légume** is masculine (despite the feminine-looking **-e** ending).
**Mange tes légumes !** — imperative of **manger** (tu form: **mange**) + possessive **tes** (your, plural object).
**Tes** = plural of **ton/ta** — used because **légumes** is plural.

**la pomme de terre** = potato, literally 'apple of the earth'. Always feminine.
Plural: **les pommes de terre** — the whole compound pluralises.
**de la purée** — partitive article for the mashed potato (uncountable quantity).
cards.phrase: Je veux de la purée de pommes de terre.

cards.grammarNote:
**la tomate** is feminine (ending in **-ate**, a common feminine pattern).
**des tomates** — partitive/indefinite plural: the salad has some tomatoes (unspecified quantity).
**a des tomates** — **avoir** + partitive for 'has/contains some': the salad has [some] tomatoes.

**l'oignon** is masculine. The spelling is irregular — the **oi** is not pronounced as usual; it sounds like 'on-yon'.
Negation: **je n'aime pas les oignons** — **ne** elides to **n'** before a vowel.
**les oignons** — definite article **les** with **aimer** expresses general dislike.

**la salade** is feminine. Can mean the salad dish OR the lettuce plant itself.
**Je vais prendre** = I'm going to have — **aller + infinitive** is the near future (futur proche).
**une salade** — indefinite article because it's a specific order (one salad), not a partitive.

**la soupe** is feminine. From Old French **soupe** (bread soaked in broth) — same root as 'supper'.
Adjective agreement: **chaude** is the feminine form of **chaud** (hot), agreeing with **la soupe**.
Partitive for serving: **de la soupe** (some soup) — useful when asking for or offering.

**le sandwich** is masculine (borrowed English word, keeps the masculine gender by default for foreign borrowings in French).
**j'ai fait** = passé composé of **faire** (to make/do): **avoir** + past participle **fait**.
**pour le déjeuner** — meals take the definite article: **le déjeuner**, **le dîner**, **le petit-déjeuner**.
cards.englishPhrase: I made a sandwich for lunch.
cards.phrase: J'ai fait un sandwich pour le déjeuner.

cards.grammarNote:
**le gâteau** is masculine. Plural: **les gâteaux** (the x replaces s in the plural — a pattern for words ending in **-eau**: bateau → bateaux, chapeau → chapeaux).
**du gâteau** — partitive: do you want some cake? (unspecified amount, not a whole cake).

**le biscuit** = biscuit/cookie. From Latin **bis coctus** (twice cooked) — same etymology as English 'biscuit'.
**ces biscuits** — demonstrative adjective **ces** (these/those, plural) agrees with the masculine plural noun.
**sont délicieux** — adjective **délicieux** is masculine plural, agreeing with **les biscuits**.

**le chocolat** is masculine. From Nahuatl **xocolātl** via Spanish.
**J'adore** = I love (stronger than **j'aime**) — no partitive needed with **adorer/aimer** + definite article for general statements.
**le chocolat** here = chocolate in general (definite article for category love).

**le sucre** is masculine. **sans sucre** — the preposition **sans** (without) always drops the article: **sans sucre**, **sans lait**, **sans gluten**.
In a café or restaurant, 'un sucre' can mean one sugar cube: **un café avec un sucre**.

**le sel** is masculine. From Latin **sal** — root of 'salary' (Roman soldiers were sometimes paid in salt).
**Tu peux me passer le sel ?** — indirect object pronoun **me** (to me) before the verb: **me passer**.
This is a polite way to ask — more direct: **Passe-moi le sel !** (imperative).
cards.phrase: Tu peux me passer le sel ?

cards.grammarNote:
**le poivre** = pepper (the spice, masculine). **le poivron** = bell pepper (a different word).
**Tu peux me passer** — same indirect object pronoun structure as with **le sel**.
Note: in French cooking, **sel et poivre** (salt and pepper) is the standard order, same as English.
cards.phrase: Tu peux me passer le poivre ?

cards.grammarNote:
**le beurre** is masculine. From Latin **butyrum**, from Greek **βούτυρον**.
**du beurre** — partitive article: I put some butter (unspecified amount) on my bread.
**sur mon pain** — possessive **mon** (my, masculine) + **pain** (m.). **mets** = present tense of **mettre** (to put), an irregular verb.
cards.englishPhrase: I put butter on my bread.
cards.phrase: Je mets du beurre sur mon pain.

cards.grammarNote:
**l'huile** is feminine — the h is aspirate so liaison/elision applies: **de l'huile**, not *de la huile*.
**de l'huile d'olive** — partitive (de + l') then a noun+de+noun compound without article.
**Cuisinez** = the vous imperative of **cuisiner** (to cook, a regular -er verb).

**le café** = coffee (the drink) AND the café (the establishment).
**le matin** = in the morning (definite article with time expressions for habitual action: **le soir**, **le week-end**).
**J'aime le café** — definite article **le** with **aimer** = general preference (I like coffee in general).
cards.englishPhrase: I like coffee in the morning.

cards.grammarNote:
**le thé** is masculine. The accent aigu (é) indicates the /e/ vowel sound.
**du thé** — partitive article: **Tu veux du thé ?** = would you like some tea?
Tea is less embedded in French daily culture than coffee — **un thé** is typically offered after dinner or for medicinal purposes.

**le vin** is masculine. Nasal vowel: **in** — the n is not pronounced, the vowel is nasalised.
**un verre de vin rouge** — a countable serving (un verre = one glass). No partitive here because 'a glass of' specifies the quantity.
Types: **le vin rouge** (red), **le vin blanc** (white), **le vin rosé** (rosé).
cards.englishPhrase: A glass of red wine, please.
cards.phrase: Un verre de vin rouge, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**la bière** is feminine. The grave accent on **è** signals the open /ɛ/ sound.
**Deux bières** — indefinite plural for two specific beers (countable order).
In a café: **une pression** = a draught beer (pint) · **une bouteille** = a bottled beer.

**le jus** is masculine. The s is silent in isolation but heard in liaison: **le jus_orange** (no liaison needed here since it's **jus d'orange**).
**du jus d'orange** — partitive **du** (de + le) for an unspecified quantity of juice.
**jus de** + fruit name: **jus de pomme** (apple juice), **jus d'orange** (orange juice).

**le petit-déjeuner** = breakfast, literally 'the little lunch'.
**prendre le petit-déjeuner** = to have breakfast — meals are 'taken' (**prendre**) in French, not 'eaten' or 'had'.
**à sept heures** — time with **à**: note no contraction since **heures** is feminine (à + la = à la, but **heures** doesn't need an article here).
cards.phrase: Je prends le petit-déjeuner à sept heures.

cards.grammarNote:
**le déjeuner** = lunch (also the verb **déjeuner** = to have lunch).
**On déjeune** — here **déjeuner** is used as a verb, not a noun. Same word does double duty.
**à deux heures** — France typically lunches between 12h and 14h; 'two o'clock' is late but not unusual on weekends.

**le dîner** = dinner/evening meal (also the verb **dîner** = to dine).
The circumflex on **î** reflects a historical **s**: Old French **disner**.
**Le dîner est prêt** — adjective **prêt** (ready, m.) agrees with **le dîner** (m.). Feminine: **la soupe est prête**.

**le goûter** specifically refers to the afternoon snack (~4pm), a French institution especially for children.
For a snack at any time, **un snack** or **un encas** is more common among adults.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a snack in the afternoon.

cards.grammarNote:
**le repas** = meal (masculine). Plural: **les repas** — the s is already there, so the plural looks identical in writing.
**un repas délicieux** — adjective follows the noun here (most adjectives do in French).
The traditional French meal is sacred — **le repas** often refers to the structured, multi-course event.

**la recette** is feminine (ending in **-ette**, a diminutive suffix, always feminine).
**une bonne recette** — adjective **bonne** precedes the noun (BAGS adjective rule: Goodness/quality).
**La recette** also means 'revenue' or 'receipts' in financial French — a false friend to watch for.
cards.englishPhrase: Do you have a good recipe?

cards.grammarNote:
**la saveur** = flavor/taste (literary/formal). More everyday: **le goût**.
**Quel goût tu veux ?** — **quel** (which/what, masculine) agrees with **goût** (m.).
**La saveur** is feminine (-eur words are mostly feminine, though **le bonheur**, **le malheur**, **le labeur** are exceptions).

**l'ingrédient** is masculine. Words ending in **-ient** are typically masculine.
**De quels ingrédients** — **quels** is the plural of **quel** (which/what), agreeing with the masculine plural **ingrédients**.
**tu as besoin de** = you need (avoir besoin de + noun) — note **de quels** for 'which' in a partitive context.
cards.phrase: De quels ingrédients tu as besoin ?

cards.grammarNote:
**la carte** = the full menu (order anything you like).
**le menu** in France = a set-price meal: entrée + plat + dessert.
Always ask for la carte to order freely.

**le restaurant** is masculine. From **restaurer** (to restore) — originally a restorative broth sold in Paris in the 18th century.
**Ce restaurant** — demonstrative adjective **ce** (this/that, masculine singular).
**est bien** vs. **est bon** — **bien** here means 'good' in quality/service; **bon** would imply good taste.

**la cuisine** = the kitchen (room) AND cooking/cuisine.
**La cuisine française** = French cuisine (cooking). **la cuisine** (room in the house).
**propre** = clean. Note: **propre** before a noun means 'own' (**ma propre voiture** = my own car), but after être it means 'clean'.

**le four** is masculine. From Latin **furnus** — also the root of **la fournée** (a batch of baked goods).
**au four** = in the oven — **à + le = au**: **faire cuire au four** (to bake/roast in the oven).
Adjective agreement: **chaud** (hot, masculine) agrees with **le four** (m.).

**la cuisinière** = the stove/cooker (feminine). Also means 'female cook'.
**Faites-le cuire** = imperative of **faire** (vous form) + direct object pronoun **le** before the infinitive **cuire**.
**sur les plaques** = on the burners/hobs — **les plaques** (f. pl.) means hobs/rings on a cooktop.

**le couteau** is masculine. Plural: **les couteaux** (x replaces s, like **gâteaux**).
**bien aiguisé** = well-sharpened — **bien** modifies the past participle **aiguisé** (used as adjective).
**avoir besoin d'un** — **de + un = d'un** (elision before vowel in **un**).
cards.phrase: J'ai besoin d'un couteau bien aiguisé.

cards.grammarNote:
**la fourchette** is feminine. Diminutive of **la fourche** (pitchfork) — a small fork.
The **-ette** suffix is always feminine: **la fourchette**, **la baguette**, **la serviette**.
**Je peux avoir** = can I have — **pouvoir + infinitive** (informal question by rising intonation, no inversion).

**la cuillère** is feminine. Also spelled **cuiller** (an older form).
**une cuillère à café** = a teaspoon (literally 'a coffee spoon') · **une cuillère à soupe** = a tablespoon.
**j'ai besoin d'une** — elision: **de + une** becomes **d'une** before vowel.

**l'assiette** is feminine. From Old French **asseoir** (to sit/set) — a plate is something that is 'set'.
**sale** = dirty (adjective following the noun via **être**). Before a noun **sale** also means dirty: **une sale affaire** (a dirty business).
**l'assiette** — the l' before a vowel is elision of **le** or **la** (can't tell gender from this form alone — but it's feminine).

**le bol** is masculine. In French, a bowl is used especially for breakfast (café au lait in a bowl is classic).
**Remplis le bol** — imperative of **remplir** (tu form: **remplis**), a regular -ir verb.
**de soupe** — after **remplir de** the noun has no article: **rempli de soupe**, **rempli d'eau**.

**le verre** = a glass (drinking vessel) AND the material 'glass'. Masculine.
**un verre d'eau** — the **de** after a container word drops the article: un verre **de** vin, une tasse **de** café, une bouteille **d'**eau.
cards.phrase: Un verre d'eau, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**la bouteille** is feminine. The **-eille** ending is always feminine: **la bouteille**, **la corbeille**, **la groseille**.
**une bouteille de vin** — container + **de** + noun (no article): **une bouteille de vin**, **un verre d'eau**, **une tasse de café**.
cards.phrase: Une bouteille de vin, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**la table** is feminine (from Latin **tabula**, feminine).
**Notre table** = our table — **notre** is the possessive adjective for 'our' (same form for m. and f. singular).
**est prête** — adjective **prêt** agrees with the feminine noun **la table**: **prête**.

**le serveur** = waiter (m.) / **la serveuse** = waitress (f.).
To get a waiter's attention: **Monsieur !** (not 'garçon' — that is now considered rude).
**l'addition** = the bill (la addition → l'addition). In a café: **l'addition, s'il vous plaît** or just **vous pouvez nous apporter l'addition ?**

French uses **avoir** for hunger, thirst, and physical sensations — never être:
**avoir faim** (hungry) · **avoir soif** (thirsty) · **avoir froid/chaud** (cold/hot) · **avoir peur** (afraid)

**soif** (thirst) is feminine — **la soif**. In the expression **avoir soif** it appears without an article.
**Tu as soif ?** — informal question by rising intonation; no inversion needed.
Related: **mourir de soif** (to be dying of thirst) — a very common hyperbolic expression in French.

**manger** is a regular -er verb with a spelling change: **nous mangeons** (adds **e** to keep the soft g sound before **-ons**).
**On mange !** = Let's eat! — **on** used for 'we' in casual French, takes the same verb form as **il/elle**.
With partitives: **je mange du pain**, **de la salade**, **des légumes** (some bread/salad/vegetables).

**boire** is irregular: je **bois**, tu **bois**, il **boit**, nous **buvons**, vous **buvez**, ils **boivent**.
**quelque chose à boire** = something to drink — **à + infinitive** expresses purpose after **quelque chose**.
With partitives: **boire du vin**, **boire de la bière**, **boire de l'eau**.
cards.englishPhrase: Would you like something to drink?
cards.phrase: Tu veux quelque chose à boire ?

cards.grammarNote:
**cuisiner** = to cook (general). **faire la cuisine** is an equivalent common expression.
**Elle cuisine très bien** — adverb **bien** (well) modifies the verb directly.
**Le cuisinier / la cuisinière** = the cook (person). **La cuisinière** also means the stove — same word, two meanings.

French has no single verb equivalent to 'bake' — it uses **faire cuire au four** (to cook in the oven).
**du pain** — partitive article: I bake some bread (unspecified amount).
**le dimanche** — definite article with days of the week for habitual actions: **le lundi** (every Monday), **le dimanche** (every Sunday). No article = a specific day: **dimanche** (this Sunday).
cards.phrase: Je fais cuire du pain au four le dimanche.

cards.grammarNote:
**frire** (to deep fry) is defective — it has limited forms and is rarely used in everyday speech.
**Faire revenir** is the preferred expression for sautéing onions in oil: **faites revenir** (vous imperative).
**dans l'huile** — preposition **dans** (in/into) for the medium you're cooking in.
cards.phrase: Faites revenir les oignons dans l'huile.

cards.grammarNote:
**rôtir** = to roast. The circumflex on **ô** signals a historical 's' (Old French **rostir**).
**Faites rôtir** = vous imperative of **faire** + infinitive **rôtir** (causative construction: make it roast).
**pendant une heure** — **pendant** (during/for) expresses duration of a completed or ongoing action.
cards.englishPhrase: Roast the chicken for one hour.
cards.phrase: Faites rôtir le poulet pendant une heure.

cards.grammarNote:
**couper** = to cut (general). **Hacher** = to chop/mince finely (as in **Hachez les oignons**).
Note the example uses **hachez** not **coupez** — in culinary contexts **hacher** implies finely chopping.
**Hachez** = vous imperative of **hacher** (regular -er verb).

**couper en tranches** = to cut into slices. **une tranche** = a slice (feminine).
**Tranchez** = vous imperative of **trancher** (to slice) — a more specific verb than **couper**.
**le pain** — definite article: slice the (specific) bread (already established in context).

**mélanger** = to mix. Like **manger**, it adds **e** before **-ons**: **nous mélangeons**.
**Mélangez** = vous imperative.
**tous les ingrédients** — **tous** (all, masculine plural) + **les ingrédients**. Pronounced 'tooss': the s is pronounced before a vowel in **tous** when it's a pronoun, but silent when it's an adjective.

**remuer** = to stir, to move, to shake. A regular -er verb.
**Remuez** = vous imperative.
**la soupe** — definite article: referring to a specific soup (the one you're making).

**fouetter** = to whisk, to beat, to whip. Also used figuratively: **la pluie fouette les vitres** (the rain lashes the windows).
**Battez** = vous imperative of **battre** (to beat) — a more common alternative to **fouetter** for eggs.
**les œufs** — plural of **l'œuf**: note the silent f in the plural.

**verser** = to pour. A regular -er verb.
**Versez l'eau** — direct object **l'eau** (definite article — the specific water you have).
**dans le verre** — **dans** (into/in) for pouring into a container. Compare: **sur** (onto): **versez sur le gâteau**.
cards.englishPhrase: Pour the water into the glass.

cards.grammarNote:
**servir** = to serve. Irregular -ir verb: je **sers**, tu **sers**, il **sert**, nous **servons**.
**a servi** = passé composé with **avoir**: **avoir** + past participle **servi**.
**le serveur** (waiter) comes from **servir** — the person who serves.
cards.phrase: Le serveur a servi la nourriture.

cards.grammarNote:
**nourrir** = to feed, to nourish. From Latin **nutrire** — same root as English 'nutrition'.
The example uses **donner à manger à** = to give food to (a more colloquial alternative to **nourrir**).
**au bébé** — indirect object with **à**: gives food **to** the baby. **à + le = au**.
cards.phrase: Elle donne à manger au bébé.

cards.grammarNote:
**dîner** = to dine / to have dinner. Also the noun **le dîner** (dinner).
**on a dîné** = passé composé with **avoir** + past participle **dîné**.
**dans un beau restaurant** — **dans** (in) for being inside an establishment. Note **beau** (beautiful, masculine) precedes the noun.
cards.englishPhrase: We dined at a nice restaurant.
cards.phrase: On a dîné dans un beau restaurant.

cards.grammarNote:
**mâcher** = to chew. The circumflex on **â** reflects a historical long vowel.
**Mâche** = tu imperative of **mâcher**.
**lentement** = slowly — adverb formed by adding **-ment** to the feminine adjective **lente** (slow, f.).

**avaler** = to swallow. A regular -er verb.
**Avalez** = vous imperative.
**avec de l'eau** — partitive **de l'** (de + l') for an unspecified amount of water. Used to take a pill with water.
cards.phrase: Avalez le comprimé avec de l'eau.

cards.grammarNote:
**assaisonner** = to season (food). Related to **la saison** (season), but in cooking it means to add seasoning.
**Assaisonnez** = vous imperative.
**du sel et du poivre** — partitive **du** (de + le) for both salt and pepper (uncountable/unspecified amounts).
cards.phrase: Assaisonnez avec du sel et du poivre.

cards.grammarNote:
**frais** (m.) / **fraîche** (f.) — an irregular feminine form.
**Le pain est frais** — adjective after **être** agrees with subject: **le pain** (m.) → **frais**.
For a woman or feminine noun: **la crème est fraîche**. **Frais** also means 'cool' in weather: **il fait frais** (it's cool/fresh outside).

**mûr** (m.) / **mûre** (f.) — the circumflex on **û** marks a historical vowel length.
**Les tomates sont mûres** — feminine plural agreement: **mûre** + **s**.
**Mûr** can also mean 'mature' of a person: **un homme mûr** (a mature man).

**cuit** = past participle of **cuire** (to cook), used as an adjective meaning 'cooked'.
**Le poulet est cuit ?** — **cuit** is masculine, agreeing with **le poulet**. Feminine: **la viande est cuite**.
Degrees of doneness: **bien cuit** (well-done), **à point** (medium), **saignant** (rare — literally 'bleeding').

**chaud** (m.) / **chaude** (f.) — adjective agrees with its noun.
**La soupe est chaude** — feminine agreement with **la soupe**.
For people and sensations use **avoir**: **j'ai chaud** (I'm hot), not *je suis chaud* (which has slang meanings to avoid).
cards.englishPhrase: Be careful, the soup is hot.

cards.grammarNote:
**froid** (m.) / **froide** (f.) — adjective agrees with its noun.
**Mon café est froid** — **froid** is masculine, agreeing with **le café** (m.).
For body temperature use **avoir**: **j'ai froid** (I'm cold). For weather: **il fait froid** (it's cold). Never *je suis froid* for temperature.

**sucré** = sweet (literally 'sugared') — past participle of **sucrer** used as adjective.
**Ce gâteau est très sucré** — **sucré** is masculine, agreeing with **le gâteau** (m.).
Contrast: **le sucre** (the noun, sugar) vs. **sucré** (the adjective, sweet/sugary). **Un dessert sucré** = a sweet dessert.

**aigre** = sour/sharp (same form for m. and f.). **acide** is used in the example (acidic/sour).
**Ce citron** — demonstrative **ce** (masculine singular before consonant).
Flavour vocabulary: **aigre** (sour) · **acide** (acidic) · **amer** (bitter) · **sucré** (sweet) · **salé** (salty) · **épicé** (spicy).

**amer** (m.) / **amère** (f.) — an irregular feminine form with a grave accent.
**Ce café est amer** — **amer** is masculine, agreeing with **le café** (m.).
Feminine example: **la bière est amère** (the beer is bitter). **L'amertume** = bitterness (noun, feminine).

**épicé** (m.) / **épicée** (f.) — past participle of **épicer** (to spice) used as an adjective.
**La cuisine mexicaine est souvent épicée** — feminine agreement: **cuisine** (f.) → **épicée**.
**souvent** (often) = adverb modifying the verb/adjective. Note **-tion** words like **la cuisine** are not **-tion** words — but all **-tion** nouns are feminine in French.
cards.phrase: La cuisine mexicaine est souvent épicée.

cards.grammarNote:
**tendre** = tender/soft (same form for masculine and feminine — adjectives ending in **-e** don't change for gender).
**La viande est tendre** — no gender change needed here.
**Tendre** can also mean 'to hold out' or 'tender-hearted' as an adjective of character: **un regard tendre** (a tender look).

**délicieux** (m.) / **délicieuse** (f.) — adjectives ending in **-eux** change to **-euse** in the feminine.
**C'est délicieux !** — **c'est** + adjective: when commenting on something without naming it, use the invariable **c'est** + masculine adjective.
For a named feminine noun: **cette tarte est délicieuse**.

**savoureux** (m.) / **savoureuse** (f.) — follows the **-eux/-euse** pattern.
**La soupe est très savoureuse** — feminine agreement with **la soupe** (f.).
**Savoureux** comes from **la saveur** (flavor). Related English words: 'savory', 'savor'.

**fade** = bland/tasteless (same form for m. and f. — ends in **-e** so no gender change).
**Le riz est fade** — **fade** is invariable for gender; for plural: **les légumes sont fades**.
**Insipide** is a more formal/literary synonym. In everyday speech **ça manque de goût** (it lacks flavor) is also common.

**sain** (m.) / **saine** (f.) = healthy.
The example uses **bons pour la santé** (good for health) — a very common alternative to **sains**.
**la santé** = health (feminine). **Être en bonne santé** = to be in good health. **À votre santé !** = Cheers! (literally 'to your health!').
cards.phrase: Les fruits sont bons pour la santé.

cards.grammarNote:
**mauvais pour la santé** = unhealthy, literally 'bad for health'.
**La restauration rapide** = fast food — a formal/official term. **Le fast-food** (borrowed) is also used informally.
**mauvaise** — feminine agreement: **la restauration** (f.) → **mauvaise**.
cards.phrase: La restauration rapide est mauvaise pour la santé.

cards.grammarNote:
**végétarien** (m.) / **végétarienne** (f.) — double n in the feminine form.
**Je suis végétarien(ne)** — with **être**, nationality/religion/profession adjectives have no article.
Compare: **je suis médecin** (I'm a doctor) — no article before profession after **être**.

**vegan** is borrowed from English and used in French without change. Pronounced approximately 'veh-gan'.
**Elle est vegan** — no article after **être** with borrowed lifestyle adjectives.
A French alternative: **végétalien(ne)** (plant-based, no animal products at all). **Vegan** is more colloquial.

**congelé** (m.) / **congelée** (f.) — past participle of **congeler** (to freeze), used as adjective.
Note the example uses **surgelé** — a more specific term for commercially frozen food. **Congelé** is food you froze at home.
**Sors** = tu imperative of **sortir** (to take out/go out), an irregular -ir verb.

**bio** = organic (abbreviation of **biologique**). Invariable — does not change for gender or number: **des légumes bio**, **une pomme bio**.
**Je préfère** = I prefer — regular -er verb. Accent changes: **préfère** (I prefer), **préférons** (we prefer).
In France, **le label AB** (Agriculture Biologique) certifies organic products.

**fait maison** = homemade, literally 'made [at] home'. Invariable — never changes form.
In France, restaurants are legally required to mark dishes as **fait maison** when made from scratch on the premises.
**J'adore** = I love (stronger than **j'aime**). **le pain fait maison** — definite article for the concept in general.

**quotidien** (m.) / **quotidienne** (f.) = daily. From Latin **quotidianus**.
**tous les jours** is used in the example (every day) — more natural in speech than **quotidiennement** (daily, as an adverb).
**Je mange des fruits** — partitive/indefinite plural **des** (some fruits — unspecified quantity).
cards.phrase: Je mange des fruits tous les jours.

cards.grammarNote:
**presque** = almost, nearly. Invariable adverb.
**Le déjeuner est presque prêt** — **presque** modifies the adjective **prêt** (ready).
**prêt** is masculine, agreeing with **le déjeuner** (m.). Feminine: **la quiche est presque prête**.

**trop** = too / too much. **trop de** + noun (no article): **trop de sel**, **trop d'eau**, **trop de sucre**.
**il y a trop de sel** — **il y a** = there is/are (invariable expression).
Contrast: **trop** (too much) vs. **très** (very): **très salé** (very salty) vs. **trop salé** (too salty).
cards.phrase: Il y a trop de sel.
